director.summary: Week 24 2026 presents a divergent performance across the estate. Skerries demonstrates strong momentum with +2.8% total sales and robust growth in edible groceries (+9.1%), supported by seasonal tourist footfall and solid own-brand penetration (+4.7%). Conversely, Palmerstown (-1.8% weekly, -4.5% YTD) and Balbriggan (-2.3% weekly, -3.1% YTD) are under pressure, with margin compression across meat/fish (-3.4 to -4.2 margin pts) and packaged bakery decline (-14-16%). The horsepower is at Skerries; Dublin stores are losing velocity despite resilient deli performance. Calendar impact (Easter 2025 fell earlier) distorts YoY comparisons, but underlying weakness in fresh categories and serveover margins demands urgent corrective action at 356 and 374.

stores.name: Palmerstown
stores.summary: Declining sales momentum (-1.8% weekly, -4.5% YTD) with significant margin erosion in meat (-3.4 pts), fish (-7.7 pts), and packaged bakery (-3.3 pts). Lunchtime trade erosion evident in midday spend decline (-€0.92 per transaction vs LY). Own-brand growth (+8.3%) insufficient to offset brand-led weakness. Urgent intervention required on category profitability and promotional strategy.

stores.name: Balbriggan
stores.summary: Moderate decline (-2.3% weekly, -3.1% YTD) but less severe than Palmerstown. Fish serveover under significant pressure (-30.3%), partially offset by deli strength (+12.5%). Chilled fruit weakness (-15.5%) suggests temperature/spoilage issue or demand shift. Own-brand stable (+1.6%). Bakery decline (-6.9%) aligns with estate trend. Requires focused category recovery plan.

stores.name: Skerries
stores.summary: Strong outperformer with +2.8% weekly sales growth and +0.8% YTD resilience in volatile environment. Edible grocery surge (+9.1% YTD) and produce growth (+2.9%) reflect seasonal tourist strength and strong local basket composition. Fish serveover +20.7% (coastal advantage), organic produce +36.1%. Deli decline (-9.0%) and loose sausages (-49%) require investigation but overall momentum is positive.

drivers.text: Skerries edible grocery momentum (+9.1% YTD, +3.6% weekly) driven by tourist footfall and seasonal prepared food demand (organic +36%, fresh bakery +12% at Balbriggan).
Deli & Food-to-Go category holding firm at Palmerstown (+4.6% weekly) and Balbriggan (+12.5% weekly) with hot food driving gains; sandwiches and hot beverages delivering margin uplift.
Meat/fish category under severe margin compression across all stores (-3.4 to -7.7 margin pts); beef serveover +46% at Palmerstown suggests heavy promotional activity eroding profitability.
Packaged bakery collapse (-14 to -16% across estate) indicates category reset or competitive loss; fresh bakery upside (+12% Palmerstown, +12% Balbriggan) suggests shift toward higher-margin fresh.

Fish serveover volatility (Balbriggan -30.3%, Skerries +20.7%) suggests supply/quality inconsistency or stock-out issues; waste data at Balbriggan dairy shows impulse confectionery waste 2.3% (elevated).
Lunchtime traffic decline at Palmerstown (midday avg spend -€0.92/txn) may reflect commuter shift post-hybrid work adoption; 10AM-Midday transaction softness (-35 to +38 variance) signals volatility.

priorities: Stabilise meat/fish margin decay: audit promotional calendar and vendor rebates (Palmerstown beef serveover +46% sale but -3.2 margin pts suggests loss-leading).
Arrest packaged bakery decline via fresh bakery migration & planogram reset; investigate Balbriggan chilled fruit -15.5% (spoilage or demand issue?).
Capitalise on Skerries tourist season momentum: increase prepared produce/deli stock, test premium organic line extension (Skerries organic +36%).
Recover Palmerstown lunchtime trade: audit deli sandwich margins (Palmerstown -16.8% sales but +5.8 margin pts = margin-protecting exit), re-engage commuter via speed/freshness.
Resolve Balbriggan fish volatility: conduct category health check (waste, holdover, supplier performance); tighten inventory discipline.

leaks.title: Beef serveover promotional bleed at Palmerstown
leaks.impact: €1,431 incremental sales but -€120-150 margin erosion (scanning at 27.5% vs 30.7% LY); if sustained weekly, €520-650 annual margin leakage
leaks.fix: Review promotional buy-in depth; enforce 24% promotional cap on serveover; revert to vendor-funded TSA if margin not recovered within 2 weeks; audit competitor pricing weekly.

leaks.title: Fish serveover structural decline (Balbriggan -30.3%, -€398k)
leaks.impact: €398/week × 52 weeks = €20.7k annual sales loss plus margin deterioration (-1.1 pts); waste tracking shows no elevated shrink so likely demand/quality issue
leaks.fix: Commission supplier quality audit (holdover/spec compliance); test reduced-assortment model (focus SKUs); increase fresh-catch signage; consider counter-service staff retraining on upsell.

leaks.title: Packaged cake category freefall (-14 to -16% across estate)
leaks.impact: Combined loss €1,101 weekly (€57k annualised); margin holding (-1.7 to -3.3 pts) indicates failed promotional recovery; impulse confectionery waste at Balbriggan elevated (2.3%)
leaks.fix: Conduct planogram reset: reduce packaged SKU count by 15%, expand fresh cake facings; test price-hold on premium vs promotional tier; audit expiry dates (spoilage risk).

leaks.title: Palmerstown lunchtime traffic erosion (midday spend -€0.92/txn)
leaks.impact: 10AM-1PM zone seeing -35 transactions vs LY despite +38 variance at 10-11AM; if replicated daily, ~£150-200/week lost lunch revenue; deli sandwich -16.8% sales supports shift
leaks.fix: Audit commute patterns (post-hybrid WFH?); revive lunchtime meal-deal bundles (deli + drink + snack); increase grab-and-go signage; test extended deli hours Mon-Fri 11:30AM-2:30PM with staffing.

leaks.title: Loose sausages/bacon inventory mismatch (Skerries -49% sales)
leaks.impact: €53/week decline; high-margin category (39.8%) so €21-26 margin impact weekly (€1,095 annualised); suggests stock-out or delisting without replacement
leaks.fix: Verify stock-out vs planogram removal at Skerries; if demand exists, restore supply; if deletion, replace with branded equivalent to preserve margin.

urgent.text: Palmerstown meat margin emergency: beef serveover generating high volume (+46%) at 27.5% margin vs 30.7% LY (€120 weekly leakage). Suspend/moderate promotion, contact vendor for rebate top-up, review competitor pricing. Owner accountability required by COB Thursday.

Balbriggan fish serveover inventory check: -30.3% decline suggests supply constraint, quality issue, or SKU mismatch. Conduct physical audit of holdover stock, check supplier delivery SLA, test temporary price reduction to drive velocity. Review by Friday close.

today: Escalate Palmerstown meat margin to Category Director (meat@moriartygroup.ie); request 7-day promotional calendar review and vendor rebate reconciliation.
Audit packaged cake waste at Balbriggan: check expiry dates, rotate stock, reduce order quantity by 10% pending fresh bakery planogram rebalance.
Schedule Skerries tourist season opportunity workshop: map local events (June-Aug), plan prepared produce/deli promotional calendar, test organic line uplift.
Conduct Palmerstown lunchtime traffic root-cause analysis: pull POS data on transaction timing, check commute pattern trends, draft meal-deal bundle proposal for next planogram cycle.
Contact all three store managers: request chilled fruit quality feedback (Balbriggan -15.5%), confirm loose sausages stock status at Skerries, validate deli waste recording.
Issue estate-wide Produce health check directive: verify chilled fruit shelf-life discipline, check 'keep-fresh' temperature compliance, request spoilage rate by category by EOW.

compliance.text: Balbriggan impulse confectionery waste elevated at 2.3% of sales (€58 weekly); exceeds system tolerance (est. 1.2-1.5%). Verify waste logging accuracy and implement 48-hour hold-for-markdown protocol on expiring stock.

Dairy waste tracking incomplete at Palmerstown and Skerries (null fields in impulse categories); non-compliant with Pyramid KPI requirements. Reissue waste-recording SOP and schedule manager training on Thu/Fri; weekly audit until 100% data capture achieved.

cross.title: Fresh bakery margin resilience despite packaged decline
cross.why: Fresh bakery performing (+12% Palmerstown, +12% Balbriggan, -2.3% Skerries) while packaged cake collapses (-14 to -16%). Suggests customer migration to artisanal/premium and margin recovery opportunity (fresh bakery 40-51% vs packaged 20%).
cross.action: Rebalance bakery planogram: reduce packaged SKUs by 20%, increase fresh bakery facings, test premium fresh cake promotion bundled with coffee (deli cross-sell). Target +€500/week margin uplift across estate by end Q2.

cross.title: Deli & Food-to-Go offsetting fresh food weakness
cross.why: Deli category up +4.6% to +12.5% across stores, with hot food driving gains (Palmerstown +14.4%, Balbriggan +7.7%, Skerries +4.4%), while fresh produce soft. Indicates daypart shift toward convenience and 'ready-to-eat' trend.
cross.action: Audit deli labour scheduling: increase mid-day shift at Palmerstown (lunchtime erosion signal), expand hot-food assortment at Skerries (tourist demand), test 'meal-build' bundles (hot food + salad + drink) to cross-sell prepared produce and lift produce category.

cross.title: Own-brand penetration variance across stores (Palmerstown +8.3% vs Balbriggan +1.6%)
cross.why: Palmerstown own-brand +8.3% ($8.3k), Skerries +4.7%, Balbriggan only +1.6%. Suggests Palmerstown has better planogram visibility or customer receptivity; Balbriggan under-indexing indicates shelf-space or promotional opportunity gap.
cross.action: Conduct own-brand planogram audit: compare facings/shelf position vs Palmerstown baseline; increase Balbriggan own-brand grocery edible allocation by 15%, run cross-promotional bundles (own-brand + branded staples) for 3 weeks, target +€2-3k weekly lift at 374.

cross.title: Seasonal/tourist driver advantage at Skerries extending to all categories
cross.why: Skerries outperforming on sales growth (+2.8% weekly), edible grocery (+9.1% YTD), produce (+2.9%), fish (+20.7%), organic (+36%) vs Dublin stores in decline. Coastal location and June school holidays (start of summer tourist season) evident.
cross.action: Develop 'Coastal Summer' promotional campaign for Skerries (June-Aug): feature BBQ items (meats, produce, deli), increase perishable stock depth for weekend footfall, test premium prepared food (deli salads, hot-smoked fish), model incremental sales +€1-2k/week, apply winning tactics to Balbriggan (coastal parity strategy).

diagnosis.wrong: Meat & Fish margin structural decay (compressed 3.4-7.7 pts across estate) masking volume growth, and packaged bakery collapse (-14 to -16% sales), combined with lunchtime traffic softening at Palmerstown, indicates loss of category profitability and daypart fragmentation.
diagnosis.why: Root causes: (1) Aggressive promotional posturing on beef serveover (Palmerstown +46% volume at -3.2 margin pts) without vendor support, (2) Packaged cake category reaching end-of-lifecycle; fresh bakery migration underway but planogram not yet optimised, (3) Post-hybrid work commute pattern shift reducing Palmerstown lunchtime footfall by ~35 txn, (4) Fish supply or quality issues at Balbriggan (-30.3%) affecting confidence. Easter 2025 calendar shift (earlier than 2026) distorts YoY but underlying trend is margin-negative volume and category reset required.
diagnosis.fix: Immediate (this week): (A) Suspend beef serveover promotion at Palmerstown; contact vendor to negotiate rebate or revert to recommended retail. (B) Audit fish supplier SLA at Balbriggan; test temporary promotional pricing to recover velocity. (C) Conduct waste audit on packaged cake and chilled fruit; reduce order quantities pending planogram reset. Medium-term (2-3 weeks): (D) Rebalance bakery planogram estate-wide: -20% packaged SKU count, +30% fresh bakery facings, bundle fresh cake with hot beverages (deli cross-sell). (E) Launch Palmerstown lunchtime recovery: introduce meal-deal bundles, extend deli hours, target +€150-200/week in 10AM-1PM zone. (F) Capitalise on Skerries seasonal momentum: expand prepared produce/premium deli, test organic line upsell, model for Balbriggan replication.

waste.summary: Across the three Moriarty Group stores, total waste reaches approximately €2,847 in euro value for the week of 2026-06-19. Palmerstown (356) leads with €1,171.42 in waste, followed by Balbriggan (374) at €830.76 and Skerries (424) at €844.82. The primary waste drivers are fresh produce (particularly loose items and salad kits), bakery products (especially baguettes and specialty breads), and prepared meals. Critical ordering and demand forecasting issues are evident across multiple product lines, with several items showing high waste but negligible retail sales—indicating systematic overordering or poor shelf-life management.
waste.summary_continued: Markdown dependency is a secondary but significant issue, particularly with ready-to-eat meals and fresh prepared salads where reduction quantities approach or exceed retail quantities. Cross-store analysis reveals consistent problem products (Demi Baguettes, Chopped Burger Salad Kit, Rocket & Parmesan Salad) indicating chain-wide ordering misalignment rather than localized issues. Immediate intervention required on high-value waste items like prepared meals, premium salads, and specialty breads to protect margin and sustainability.

stores.name: Palmerstown

top_offenders.insight: Critical ordering failure: 15 units wasted with only 13 at full price. High markdown dependency (5 units reduced). Retail Qty 13 vs waste 15 indicates systematic overproduction. Recommend 30% order reduction and implement daily demand tracking for prepared meals.

Severe markdown dependency: 12 reduction qty equals 43% of retail sales. Product is margin-destructive. Consider discontinuing or reducing order to 50% of current levels. All units either sold at discount or wasted.

Extreme ordering failure: 11 units wasted with only 1 unit at full price. 1100% waste-to-retail ratio. Immediate action required—reduce orders to 2 units maximum and validate demand before restocking.

top_offenders.product: SV SIG TASTES GREEK SALAD BOWL | 185GRM
top_offenders.insight: Extreme overordering with markdown crisis: 13 waste + 7 reductions (20 units) vs 9 retail. 69% of prepared units discounted or wasted. Unviable product at this store—reduce to 5 units and monitor closely.

Catastrophic failure: 12 units wasted with only 1 sold at full price. 1200% waste-to-retail ratio. This is a chain-wide problem (see Balbriggan). Recommend immediate order halt until demand validation completed.

stores.category_breakdown: Prepared Meals & Salads (€230+ waste) are the primary category driver, representing 20% of total waste. Bakery products (Demi Baguettes €44.80, miscellaneous breads) contribute €90+. Produce including bananas, peppers, greens, and loose fruit accounts for €140+. Meat products (Steak & Kidney Pie, Chicken Wings, Sausage Rolls) contribute €110+. The pattern indicates overreliance on visual ordering rather than POS-driven demand forecasting.
stores.action: URGENT: Halt overordering of prepared meals and fresh salads immediately. Implement daily demand review for items with waste-to-retail ratios exceeding 50%. Specifically: reduce Roast Dinner orders by 30%, eliminate Fitfoods until demand validated, cap Greek Salad and Rocket Salad at 2-3 units daily. Introduce 48-hour shelf-life clear-and-mark protocol for all prepared items to maximize recovery before waste.

stores.name: Balbriggan

top_offenders.insight: Catastrophic confectionery overstock: 70 units wasted with only 5 retail sales. 1400% waste-to-retail ratio. Zero markdown attempt suggests late-discovery of expiration. Implement FIFO discipline and reduce SKU stocking to 10 units maximum. Consider delisting this product.

Chain-wide bakery issue (also Palmerstown 16 waste). Despite 139 retail sales, 40 units wasted (22% waste rate). Excessive production orders. Recommend reducing daily bake quantity by 25% and implementing dynamic end-of-day discount protocol. Low markdown ratio suggests poor markdown discipline.

Confectionery overstock: 20 wasted vs 3 sold. 667% waste-to-retail ratio. Premium-priced item with poor turnover. Reduce to impulse quantities (5 units) or discontinue. No markdown attempted despite obvious overstocking.

Produce quality/freshness issue: 19 waste with modest retail (16). 54% waste rate suggests ripeness management problem. Implement closer temperature control or reduce order to 20 units and validate shelf-life tracking weekly.

Chain-wide problem confirmed: 12 waste + 7 reductions vs 15 retail sales. 56% of units non-revenue or waste. Combined with Palmerstown data, this SKU is unviable. Discontinue across group immediately.

stores.category_breakdown: Confectionery & Packaged Snacks (€210+ waste including Coconut Creams, Snickers, Twirl Orange, Mars, Lindt) are the dominant category at 25% of waste. Bakery (€151.40 with Demi Baguettes and French Stick) follows closely. Produce (Peaches, Mooli, Lettuce, Carrot & Turnip) contributes €90+. Prepared Salads (House Salad, Rocket & Parmesan) account for €113+. Balbriggan shows weaker markdown discipline than Palmerstown—high-waste items show minimal reduction activity.
stores.action: URGENT: Establish weekly confectionery ordering review—Coconut Creams and Snickers are destroying margin. Reduce all non-core branded confectionery to 10-unit maximum. For Demi Baguettes, coordinate with bakery to reduce daily production by 30% and implement 16:00 markdown protocol to €1.50 for next-day items. Discontinue Chopped Burger Salad Kit store-wide. Introduce daily markdown reviews for any item showing >40% waste rate to maximize recovery.

stores.name: Skerries

top_offenders.product: HAPPY PEAR PEANUT & CRAN ENERGY BAL | 125GRM
top_offenders.insight: Premium product ordering disaster: 22 wasted vs 2 retail sales. 1100% waste-to-retail ratio with €107 loss. This is margin-destroying stock. Immediately halt ordering and investigate supplier push-back. Reduce to trial quantities (3 units) only if relisting.

Regional snack overstock: 23 wasted vs 3 sold. 767% waste-to-retail ratio. Premium regional product with poor market fit at Skerries. Reduce to 5 units max or discontinue. No markdown attempt despite obvious failure.

Produce quality issue despite decent sales: 30 waste vs 67 retail (31% waste rate). Suggests storage, temperature, or quality control problem. Implement daily inspection protocol and reduce daily delivery by 20% until shelf-life improves.

Perishable produce overstock: 20 waste vs 11 sales. 65% waste rate on premium vegetable. Excessive daily ordering for small market. Reduce to 10 units daily and implement 48-hour markdown to €1.99 threshold.
Catastrophic produce failure: 15 wasted vs 1 sold. 1500% waste-to-retail ratio. Quality, packaging, or poor product fit. Remove from range immediately. No markdown discipline evident.

stores.category_breakdown: Produce (including potatoes, asparagus, tomatoes, cucumbers, broccoli, basil) is the dominant waste driver at €240+ (28% of total), indicating systematic overordering for a smaller market store. Premium packaged snacks (Happy Pear, Keoghs, Walkers) contribute €175+ (20% of waste). Meat products (Cornfed Chicken, Pork Chops, Pudding) account for €155+. Bakery breadcrumbs and prepared salads contribute €65+. Skerries shows poorest markdown discipline—many items zero reduction activity despite extreme waste ratios.
stores.action: URGENT: Reduce produce delivery quantities by 30% immediately—Skerries market size does not justify current volume. Implement daily produce quality audits with mandatory markdown at 48-hour mark for all fresh items. Discontinue: Happy Pear Energy Balls, My Daily Snack Tomatoes, Keoghs Crisps—these show no market demand. For premium items (Asparagus, Baby Potatoes), cap daily stock at 10-12 units and review weekly. Introduce markdown discipline: establish automatic 40% reduction triggers for any item exceeding 40% waste rate.

group_actions: CHAIN-WIDE PRODUCT DISCONTINUATION: Immediately cease ordering Supervalu Chopped Burger Salad Kit (waste across Palmerstown & Balbriggan; 56%+ non-revenue rate), Happy Pear Peanut & Cran Energy Balls (1100% waste-to-retail), My Daily Snack Tomatoes (1500% waste-to-retail), and Jacobs Coconut Creams (1400% waste-to-retail). These SKUs are margin-destructive across all locations.

DEMAND FORECASTING OVERHAUL: Transition from visual/historical ordering to POS-driven 14-day rolling demand analysis for all prepared meals, fresh salads, and perishable produce. Target: reduce prepared meal waste from current 25-30% to <10% within 8 weeks. Implement weekly order review meetings between store managers and supply chain with variance analysis on all items exceeding 20% waste rates.

BAKERY & FRESH GOODS PRODUCTION DISCIPLINE: Reduce Demi Baguette orders by 25% across Palmerstown & Balbriggan (currently 22-40 unit waste weekly). Implement dynamic end-of-day markdown protocol at 16:00 for all bakery items >24 hours old: set price to 50% of retail. Establish daily production targets based on prior 7-day average sales rather than arbitrary bake quantities.

MARKDOWN RECOVERY PROGRAM: Introduce automatic markdown triggers at 48-hour mark for all fresh prepared items and perishables (salads, produce, dairy) to maximize recovery before waste occurs. Current data shows markdown quantities significantly underutilized—many products (20+ items) show high waste with zero reduction activity. Target recovery improvement of €150-200 weekly through disciplined markdown execution.

STORE-SPECIFIC RATIONALIZATION: (1) Palmerstown: Focus on prepared meals discipline; reduce Roast Dinner, Fitfoods orders by 30%. (2) Balbriggan: Confectionery audit required—reduce branded snack SKU count by 30% and implement 10-unit maximum stock caps on premium items. (3) Skerries: Right-size produce ordering for smaller market (30% reduction) and discontinue premium regional snacks showing <5 retail unit sales. Tailor assortment by store size.

SUPPLIER & LOGISTICS REVIEW: Investigate potential push-stock behavior on high-waste confectionery items (Lindt, Snickers, Cadbury variants). Cross-reference delivery quantities vs. actual demand patterns. Renegotiate delivery frequency for perishables—daily delivery for produce and prepared items rather than multi-day stock. Implement expiry date visibility at point of order.
